blocks.text: Design a Toast Notification System for a frontend system design interview. You need a global API such as toast.success(message, options?) and toast.error(message, options?), a top-level toast layer rendered through a portal, predictable stacking, auto-dismiss timers, manual dismissal, responsive placement, and accessible screen-reader announcements. This guide walks through requirements, architecture, data model, API contracts, edge cases, and senior-level tradeoffs for building a production-ready toast system.

blocks.title: Scenario
blocks.text: A user saves a profile. toast.success('Profile saved', { durationMs: 4000 }) is called. The store creates a toast with id, variant, message, placement, and duration. ToastProvider renders it through a portal. ToastItem starts an auto-dismiss timer. If the user closes it manually, the timer is cleared and the toast is removed.

blocks.title: Toast system vs notification feed
blocks.text: A toast system is for short-lived, non-blocking feedback. A notification feed is persistent, user-specific, often server-backed, and may need unread state, pagination, realtime updates, and retention. This page focuses on designing the Toast Notification System, not a persistent notifications feed.

**What you are solving:**
You need a global Toast Notification System that lets any part of the app call something like `toast.success(message, options?)` or `toast.error(message, options?)` and shows small non-blocking messages on top of the UI.

It should support different variants (success, error, warning, info), auto-dismiss after a delay, manual close, predictable stacking, placement rules, accessibility, and cleanup without breaking the page layout.

**What the interviewer looks for:**
- You understand that this is a **global concern**, not a local component.
- You talk about **stacking multiple toasts** and what happens when many are fired.
- You mention **auto-dismiss timers** and how they are cleaned up.
- You are aware of **z-index / layering** over the rest of the UI.
- You bring up **accessibility** (ARIA roles, announcements, focus) and **responsive placement**.

blocks.title: How you should act here
blocks.text: Start by restating the problem in your own words: "We want a global toast API like `toast.success(message, options?)` that shows short-lived notifications, stacks them nicely, and cleans up timers correctly." Then move into clarifying questions instead of jumping straight into implementation details.

steps.title: 1. Trigger
steps.text: Some part of the app calls `toast.success('Profile saved', { durationMs: 4000 })` after an action completes.

steps.title: 2. Show toast
steps.text: A new toast appears in the chosen corner (e.g. top-right), with the correct style and icon for its type.

steps.title: 3. Lifetime
steps.text: A timer starts for auto-dismiss. The user can also close it manually (close button or click).

steps.title: 4. Stack behavior
steps.text: If more toasts appear, they stack in a consistent order (e.g. newest on top). Old ones disappear as timers finish or the user closes them.

steps.title: 5. Edge cases
steps.text: On small screens, the layout adapts (e.g. full-width at the top/bottom). Screen readers get the message, and timers are cleaned up on unmount or route changes.

blocks.title: Smart clarifying questions you should ask
items: From where can toasts be triggered? Only from React components, or also from plain JS utilities / services?
Do we need different variants (success, error, warning, info) with different icons and colors?
What is the default auto-dismiss timeout? Can it be customized per toast?
How many toasts can be visible at the same time before we start removing or queuing them?
Which placements do we need to support (top-right, top-center, bottom-right, bottom-left, mobile full-width)?
Do we need actions inside toasts (e.g. an "Undo" button) that require focus and keyboard support?

blocks.title: What you should explicitly confirm
items: The toast system is **global** and should not require prop-drilling.
Toasts are **non-blocking**: they should not stop the user from interacting with the page.
Each toast has a **type**, **message**, and **lifetime** (auto-dismiss + manual close).
Multiple toasts can be visible and must **stack predictably**.
Timers must be **cleaned up** on unmount or when the toast is removed.
We care about **accessibility**: ARIA roles (e.g. `status`, `alert`), screen reader announcements, and sensible focus behavior for interactive toasts.

items.label: Main concept
items.helperText: One place in the app renders all toasts.

items.label: Key UX goal
items.helperText: Users keep working while seeing notifications.

items.label: Key technical concern
items.helperText: No dangling intervals/timeouts when toasts disappear or routes change.

blocks.title: Key message to land
blocks.text: A toast system is more than just "show a div". In requirements, highlight that it must be global, stack correctly, clean up timers reliably, respect z-index layers, and stay accessible and readable on both desktop and mobile.
